column4: A virtual machine is a software-defined computer that runs as a guest with its own operating system, resources, and settings on shared physical hardware.
column5:
Why

The host owns the real hardware, but each guest behaves like a separate computer. This separation lets one physical system run multiple operating systems or workloads.
column9: Professor Messer 220-1201 v1.70 p.46; CompTIA 220-1201 Exam Objectives v4.0, Objective 4.1
column10: A+::220-1201::4.1 A+::220-1201::Domain4-VirtualizationCloud A+::220-1201::VirtualizationConcepts Basic Virtualization VM Source::Messer-v170

column2: Why use a virtual machine as a sandbox?
column4: A VM sandbox isolates testing from the real production system, so changes, failures, or unsafe behavior are less likely to affect the host or production environment.
column5:
Why

Sandboxing is useful for testing code, configuration changes, or suspicious behavior. Snapshots can also help return the VM to a known state after testing.
column9: Professor Messer 220-1201 v1.70 p.46; CompTIA 220-1201 Exam Objectives v4.0, Objective 4.1
column10: A+::220-1201::4.1 A+::220-1201::Domain4-VirtualizationCloud A+::220-1201::VirtualizationConcepts Basic HighYield Virtualization Sandbox Source::Messer-v170

column2: How do virtual machines support test and development work?
column4: They provide separate environments where developers can build and test software before it reaches production.
column5:
Distinction

Development is where the application is built. Testing is where the pieces are checked together before release. VMs help keep both stages separate from production systems.
column9: Professor Messer 220-1201 v1.70 p.46; CompTIA 220-1201 Exam Objectives v4.0, Objective 4.1
column10: A+::220-1201::4.1 A+::220-1201::Domain4-VirtualizationCloud A+::220-1201::VirtualizationConcepts Basic Virtualization Development Source::Messer-v170

column2: A business app only runs correctly on an older operating system. How can virtualization help?
column4: Run the older operating system in a VM and install the legacy application inside that guest.
column5:
Why

This keeps the old application environment available without requiring the entire physical computer to run the older operating system.
column9: Professor Messer 220-1201 v1.70 p.46; CompTIA 220-1201 Exam Objectives v4.0, Objective 4.1
column10: A+::220-1201::4.1 A+::220-1201::Domain4-VirtualizationCloud A+::220-1201::VirtualizationConcepts Basic Virtualization LegacySoftware Source::Messer-v170

column4: It lets a user run operating systems or applications from different platforms on the same physical computer without rebooting between them.
column5:
Why

No single operating system runs every tool. Cross-platform virtualization lets a user move between guests, such as Windows, Linux, or macOS environments, on one host.
column9: Professor Messer 220-1201 v1.70 p.46; CompTIA 220-1201 Exam Objectives v4.0, Objective 4.1
column10: A+::220-1201::4.1 A+::220-1201::Domain4-VirtualizationCloud A+::220-1201::VirtualizationConcepts Basic HighYield Virtualization CrossPlatform Source::Messer-v170

column2: What is the purpose of a hypervisor?
column4: A hypervisor creates and manages the virtual platform for guest operating systems.
column5:
Why

The hypervisor manages virtualized CPU, memory, storage, networking, and guest operation. It is also called a virtual machine manager.
column9: Professor Messer 220-1201 v1.70 p.46; CompTIA 220-1201 Exam Objectives v4.0, Objective 4.1
column10: A+::220-1201::4.1 A+::220-1201::Domain4-VirtualizationCloud A+::220-1201::VirtualizationConcepts Basic HighYield Virtualization Hypervisor Source::Messer-v170

column2: What distinguishes a Type 1 hypervisor from a Type 2 hypervisor?
column4: A Type 1 hypervisor runs directly on the hardware as the primary platform; a Type 2 hypervisor runs on top of an existing host operating system.
column5:
Exam Trap

Type 1 is often called bare metal. Type 2 is often called hosted.
column9: Professor Messer 220-1201 v1.70 p.46; CompTIA 220-1201 Exam Objectives v4.0, Objective 4.1
column10: A+::220-1201::4.1 A+::220-1201::Domain4-VirtualizationCloud A+::220-1201::VirtualizationConcepts Basic HighYield Virtualization Hypervisor Type1 Source::Messer-v170

column2: A technician installs virtualization software as an app inside Windows. Which hypervisor type is this?
column4: Type 2, because the hypervisor is hosted by an existing operating system.
column5:
Why

Hosted hypervisors are common for desktop labs and client-side testing. The host OS starts first, and the VMs run above that host OS.
column9: Professor Messer 220-1201 v1.70 p.46; CompTIA 220-1201 Exam Objectives v4.0, Objective 4.1
column10: A+::220-1201::4.1 A+::220-1201::Domain4-VirtualizationCloud A+::220-1201::VirtualizationConcepts Basic HighYield Virtualization Hypervisor Type2 Source::Messer-v170

column2: Why must a virtualization host be sized for CPU, memory, and storage?
column4: The host platform needs suitable CPU virtualization support, enough memory for the host and guests, and enough storage for each guest's virtual disk image.
column5:
Distinction

Virtualization adds overhead. CPU virtualization support belongs to the host platform, while each guest consumes allocated CPU time, memory, storage, and virtual hardware.
column9: Professor Messer 220-1201 v1.70 p.47; CompTIA 220-1201 Exam Objectives v4.0, Objective 4.1
column10: A+::220-1201::4.1 A+::220-1201::Domain4-VirtualizationCloud A+::220-1201::VirtualizationConcepts Basic Virtualization Resources Source::Messer-v170

column2: How do NAT, bridged, and private VM networking differ?
column4: NAT shares the host's network connection, bridged networking makes the VM appear as a device on the physical network, and private networking keeps the VM inside an internal virtual network.
column5:
Distinction

Choose the network mode based on the communication requirement. Some VMs need outside access, some need to appear on the LAN, and some should remain isolated.
column9: Professor Messer 220-1201 v1.70 p.47; CompTIA 220-1201 Exam Objectives v4.0, Objective 4.1
column10: A+::220-1201::4.1 A+::220-1201::Domain4-VirtualizationCloud A+::220-1201::VirtualizationConcepts Basic HighYield Virtualization Networking Source::Messer-v170

column2: Why does each guest operating system need its own security controls?
column4: Each guest is a self-contained computer, so it still needs normal controls such as patching, firewall rules, anti-malware, and access management.
column5:
Why

Virtual machines are isolated, but isolation does not make them automatically trusted or patched. Treat each guest as a separate system to protect.
column9: Professor Messer 220-1201 v1.70 p.47; CompTIA 220-1201 Exam Objectives v4.0, Objective 4.1
column10: A+::220-1201::4.1 A+::220-1201::Domain4-VirtualizationCloud A+::220-1201::VirtualizationConcepts Basic HighYield Virtualization Security Source::Messer-v170

column2: Why are unknown or third-party virtual machine images risky?
column4: They may contain unwanted software or insecure configurations, and the user may not know what is running inside the guest.
column5:
Why

A VM image can look convenient, but it is still a complete system. Importing an untrusted image can introduce an unmanaged or malicious guest into the environment.
column9: Professor Messer 220-1201 v1.70 p.47; CompTIA 220-1201 Exam Objectives v4.0, Objective 4.1
column10: A+::220-1201::4.1 A+::220-1201::Domain4-VirtualizationCloud A+::220-1201::VirtualizationConcepts Basic Virtualization Security Source::Messer-v170

column4: VDI provides desktop sessions or applications from remote infrastructure while the client device mainly displays the session and sends user input.
column5:
Why

VDI can reduce endpoint hardware requirements because the desktop workload runs elsewhere. Hosted virtual desktops may also be described as Desktop as a Service.
column9: Professor Messer 220-1201 v1.70 p.47; CompTIA 220-1201 Exam Objectives v4.0, Objective 4.1
column10: A+::220-1201::4.1 A+::220-1201::Domain4-VirtualizationCloud A+::220-1201::VirtualizationConcepts Basic HighYield Virtualization VDI Source::Messer-v170

column2: Why is network connectivity a major requirement for VDI?
column4: The desktop session runs remotely, so the user's display, input, and application interaction depend on the network connection.
column5:
Why

VDI moves much of the desktop experience across the network. A weak connection can make an otherwise functional virtual desktop feel unusable.
column9: Professor Messer 220-1201 v1.70 p.47; CompTIA 220-1201 Exam Objectives v4.0, Objective 4.1
column10: A+::220-1201::4.1 A+::220-1201::Domain4-VirtualizationCloud A+::220-1201::VirtualizationConcepts Basic Virtualization VDI Networking Source::Messer-v170

column4: It includes the application code and dependencies needed to run the application as a portable software unit.
column5:
Distinction

A container packages the application environment, but it does not normally include a full guest operating system the way a VM does.
column9: Professor Messer 220-1201 v1.70 p.47; CompTIA 220-1201 Exam Objectives v4.0, Objective 4.1
column10: A+::220-1201::4.1 A+::220-1201::Domain4-VirtualizationCloud A+::220-1201::VirtualizationConcepts Basic HighYield Virtualization Containers Source::Messer-v170

column2: Why are containers usually lighter than full virtual machines?
column4: Containers share the host operating system kernel instead of running a separate guest operating system for each application instance.
column5:
Distinction

A VM isolates a full operating system. A container isolates an application and its dependencies while relying on the host OS kernel.
column9: Professor Messer 220-1201 v1.70 p.47; CompTIA 220-1201 Exam Objectives v4.0, Objective 4.1
column10: A+::220-1201::4.1 A+::220-1201::Domain4-VirtualizationCloud A+::220-1201::VirtualizationConcepts Basic HighYield Virtualization Containers Source::Messer-v170

column2: What security benefit does container isolation provide?
column4: It helps keep application processes separated so one containerized application cannot freely interact with another.
column5:
Why

Container isolation is useful for application separation, but the containers still depend on the host operating system and container runtime.
column9: Professor Messer 220-1201 v1.70 p.47; CompTIA 220-1201 Exam Objectives v4.0, Objective 4.1
column10: A+::220-1201::4.1 A+::220-1201::Domain4-VirtualizationCloud A+::220-1201::VirtualizationConcepts Basic Virtualization Containers Security Source::Messer-v170

column2: Why is hypervisor security important in a virtualization environment?
column4: The hypervisor manages the virtual platform and guest isolation, so a compromised hypervisor could affect multiple guest systems.
column5:
Why

The hypervisor sits between guests and the underlying hardware. Keep this card at the concept level: the exam target is why that control layer matters, not detailed exploit mechanics.
column9: Professor Messer 220-1201 v1.70 p.47; CompTIA 220-1201 Exam Objectives v4.0, Objective 4.1
column10: A+::220-1201::4.1 A+::220-1201::Domain4-VirtualizationCloud A+::220-1201::VirtualizationConcepts Basic HighYield Virtualization Hypervisor Security Source::Messer-v170
